Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic even though I tried to keep things organized. I'd usually set aside time in the evenings after work, but it never really stuck to a schedule. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams and mind maps from the lecture content, which took forever but helped me actually understand things. With limited resources, I had to be creative about where and when I could study effectively.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, logging in every day, and other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline loomed. Working with limited financial resources means I sometimes had to prioritize other things over studying, then cram everything in at once. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work somehow.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or just this anxious feeling that I was falling behind that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about new content and feel compelled to check it out immediately, even if I wasn't ready to actually engage with it. The anxiety would build up if I hadn't logged in for a day or two, so that was often the push I needed.
Q4: For a while, logging in became almost automatic—like checking my phone first thing in the morning. I think it happened because I was genuinely surprised by how much I was enjoying some of the content, which I didn't expect. But then life would get in the way and the routine would break. The CBT sessions I've been doing helped me recognize when I was avoiding things, which made me more intentional about logging in.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I'm such a visual learner. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing to sketch out concepts and make visual notes. Any content that had diagrams, flowcharts, or visual representations really clicked for me. I found those way more useful than walls of text or reading-heavy modules.
Q6: I basically never touched the discussion forums—zero posts from me. It's not that I didn't care, but the anxiety around putting my thoughts out there for everyone to judge was just too much. I also tended to skip over dense reading materials, especially PDFs that were just text without any visual elements. If something looked boring or overwhelming at first glance, I'd tell myself I'd come back to it later and then never did.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to what I needed to know for exams or assignments. Visual content that I could actually remember and use later always felt valuable. If something seemed like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grades, I struggled to justify spending time on it when I had so many other demands on my limited time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. Required work had clear consequences, so even when my anxiety was through the roof, I'd push through. Optional stuff would trigger this internal debate about whether it was worth the stress, and usually the stress won. It's weird because sometimes the optional content looked interesting, but the pressure of everything else made me feel guilty for even considering it.
Q9: Oh absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download the videos and lecture slides, then work with them on my laptop without internet. I filled notebooks with visual summaries, diagrams, and color-coded notes that I'd review constantly. A lot of my learning happened while I was commuting or during breaks at work, just flipping through my handwritten notes.
Q10: There were so many times I was deeply engaged with the material but the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours redrawing complex diagrams from memory to test myself, or explaining concepts out loud to myself while looking at my notes. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay it mentally while sketching what I remembered. That kind of active recall and visual processing was where real learning happened for me.
Q11: All the time. I'd download everything I could in one session, then work with those materials for days without logging back in. My data situation isn't always reliable, so it made sense to grab what I needed and then study offline. I'd annotate PDFs on my laptop, screenshot important slides, and basically create my own offline study system.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt impossible. My anxiety would spike, usually around midterms or when multiple assignments overlapped, and I'd just freeze. Sometimes personal stuff—money stress, family things—would make it hard to focus on anything academic. Those were the times when even logging in felt like too much, even though I knew I should.
Q13: Completely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by everything piling up, the LMS became this thing I'd avoid because it represented all my stress. Confusion about assignment requirements would make me anxious, which made me procrastinate, which made me more anxious. It was a vicious cycle. The CBT has helped me recognize these patterns, but breaking them is still a work in progress.
Q14: So many times. I'd log in, click through some videos, maybe open a few documents, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd be physically present but mentally checked out, just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something. Sometimes I'd realize I'd watched twenty minutes of a lecture without absorbing a single thing because I was too anxious about other stuff.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety around starting something imperfect was paralyzing—I'd rather not start than start badly. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace, then that headspace would never come. It's why my assignment completion rate is so low even though I actually do well when I finally submit things.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my avoidance patterns. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming in all the LMS engagement I'd been putting off. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of a deadline cut through the anxiety paralysis in a way nothing else could. My login patterns probably looked like a heart monitor—flat lines punctuated by spikes.
Q17: Getting good feedback was actually surprising and motivating—I'd feel this rush of relief and validation that would carry me for a while. But if feedback was critical or even just neutral, my anxiety would spiral and I'd avoid logging in for days. It's like I needed to emotionally prepare myself before checking grades or comments, which meant I often delayed looking at feedback even when it could have helped me.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and visual content worked well for my learning style. But the flexibility that was supposed to be helpful sometimes made things harder because I'd use it as permission to procrastinate. Having some structure helped, but I think I needed more external accountability than the course provided. The peer interaction opportunities were there, but my anxiety kept me from using them.
Q19: The visual elements and video content made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was anything that required public participation, like discussion posts—that triggered my anxiety immediately. Also, when assignment instructions were vague or text-heavy, I'd get overwhelmed and avoid them. Clearer, more visual instructions would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. High activity can mean someone is anxious and checking things constantly without actually producing work. Or they're engaging deeply with content but struggling with the final step of submitting something. The fear of being judged on submitted work is different from the safety of just consuming content. Sometimes clicking around feels productive even when it isn't.
Q21: That also sounds like me in some ways—I did well on exams despite not having huge LMS activity because so much of my studying happened offline. If you understand the material deeply through your own methods, you don't necessarily need to be constantly logged in. Some people learn better through their own systems than through platform engagement. The grades reflect learning, not clicking.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, time spent on pages. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and what's happening in someone's head. They can't see if I'm actually learning or just anxiously clicking around. They miss all the offline work, the mental processing, the anxiety that might be driving or preventing engagement. It's just surface-level data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just getting through it. It's when I'm curious and thinking about concepts even when I'm not actively studying. For me, it's those moments when I'm drawing diagrams and suddenly something clicks—that's real engagement. It's not about how many times I log in; it's about whether the learning is actually happening in my brain.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety levels or how much emotional energy it takes me to do simple things like submit an assignment. It can't capture the hours I spent with my handwritten notes, the visual maps I created, or how I'd quiz myself during my commute. It also can't show that I genuinely found the material interesting and surprising, even when my engagement looked inconsistent. My internal experience of learning was way richer than my digital footprint suggests.
